sections.paragraph:
φιλάδελφος, ον (for the prim. sense ‘loving one’s brother/ sister’ in ref. to siblings s. φιλαδελφία; Soph., X.+; on gravestones [Sb 6234; 6235; 6653]; 2 Macc 15:14; 4 Macc 13:21; 15:10; Philo, De Jos. 218) in our literature only in the transf. sense having affection for an associate, having brotherly love, having mutual affection (cp. Socrat., Ep. 28, 12=sociable; 2 Macc 15:14=loving one’s compatriots) 1 Pt 3:8.—New Docs 3, 87 (ins reff., incl. one fr. Bithynia: TAM IV, 111). M-M. TW.

prim. prim. = primarily

ref. ref. = reference(s)

Soph Soph , V b.c.—List 5

  1. X. = Xenophon, V–IV b.c.—List 5

Sb Sb = Sammelbuch griechischer Urkunden aus Aegypten—Lists 3, 4

Philo Philo = P. of Alexandria, I b.c.–I a.d.—List 5

transf. transf. = transferred

  1. cp. = compare, freq. in ref. to citation fr. ancient texts

Ep.

Ep. = Epistola/Epistula, when applied to letters mostly pseudonymous; various dates—List 5

  1. = epistle

ins Ins, ins = Inscription, Inschrift, inscription(s). Without a period, esp. in lists, as at the beginning of entries; the capitalized form is used in titles. In conjunction with literary works this abbr. refers to the title or description of contents.

incl. incl. = including

  1. fr. = from

TAM TAM = Tituli Asiae Minoris—List 3

M-M M-M = JMoulton/GMilligan, Vocabulary of Greek Testament—Lists 4, 6

TW TW = Theologisches Wörterbuch zum NT; tr. GBromiley, Theological Dictionary of the NT—List 6

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 1055
